metrics_ignored: Historical peak performance during outliers (e.g., post-pandemic revenge spending)
data.logic_rule: IF real-world observations (e.g., visible boycotts or unusual promotional activity) indicate a decline in consumer demand, THEN anticipate significant revenue contraction and potential quarterly losses before the official earnings release.
data.embedding_summary: Retail and F&B investors can use 'boots on the ground' observations of store traffic and discount strategies to predict earnings misses. Social or political boycotts serve as a leading indicator of revenue drops that may not yet be reflected in the stock price.

metrics_ignored: Short-term price volatility of the parent company prior to subsidiary announcements
data.logic_rule: IF key profit-contributing subsidiaries report record-breaking or significantly improved earnings, THEN the parent company's financial results and stock price are highly likely to follow a similar positive trajectory.
data.embedding_summary: The performance of a holding company is a direct function of its major subsidiaries. Investors can deduce the parent company's upcoming results by analyzing the already-released earnings of its primary business units, exploiting the correlation between them.

metrics_ignored: Specific company-level news that doesn't affect the broader industry
data.logic_rule: IF a leading player in a consolidated industry reports record-breaking results, THEN anticipate similar performance from other major peers in the same sector who have yet to announce their earnings.
data.embedding_summary: In industries with few players (oligopolies), favorable market conditions usually benefit all participants. Investors can use the 'sector lag' effect, where an early reporter's success provides a high-probability signal for peers whose earnings reports are scheduled a few days or weeks later.

metrics_ignored: Headline net loss (if caused by one-off items)
data.logic_rule: IF a reported loss is primarily driven by a one-off divestment of a non-performing unit rather than a decline in the core business, THEN the market may view the result as fundamentally neutral or positive.
data.embedding_summary: Sophisticated investors look past the 'headline loss' to see if the core business is still profitable. Losses incurred from selling off a weak subsidiary are often ignored by the market because they clean up the balance sheet for future quarters.

metrics_used: Opportunity Window (period between account closing and reporting)
metrics_ignored: Daily market noise outside of the earnings window
data.logic_rule: IF the stock price significantly declines leading up to an earnings report (due to anticipated bad news), THEN the actual announcement of poor results may result in a price floor or recovery as the news is already 'priced in.'
data.embedding_summary: The period between a company's financial quarter-end and its official reporting date often sees 'leakage' or anticipation where insiders or observant investors sell off. If the price drops ahead of time, the actual bad news often fails to drive the price further down.
